title: Chapter I–II: The Stranger and the Decision
paragraphs: Gustav von Aschenbach—or Gustave Aschenbach, as he had been known officially since his fiftieth birthday—had set out alone from his house in Prince Regent Street, Munich, for an extended walk. It was a spring afternoon in that year of grace 19—, when Europe sat upon the anxious seat beneath a menace that hung over its head for months.

He was a man of middle height, somewhat past fifty, with darkish complexion, alert eyes, and a thin compressed mouth. He wore a neat city suit, a derby hat, and carried a stick. He walked with a measured, self-respecting gait. His work, his regular habits, his days of labor from morning to evening—these had endowed him with the stern, deliberate composure that is the mark of a man who has conquered his own nature.

He had paused to rest before the north portal of the Führich church and was standing there looking at the chapel on the opposite side of the road. Suddenly a man appeared there, strange and sudden—a man standing on the steps, reading the inscriptions. He was of more than ordinary height, slender, beardless, and strikingly snub-nosed; he belonged to the red-haired type and possessed its milky, freckled skin. He wore a straw hat and carried a stout stick. He was not of Bavarian extraction; the broad straight-brimmed straw hat, the short hair beneath it, the lean sunburnt face, the Nordic features—all this gave him a foreign look.
The man was not there long, and not entirely real—or so it seemed to Aschenbach afterward. He had stood there reading the inscription, and then was gone.

But something remained. A longing—a wanderer's longing—for the distant and the new. It came over Aschenbach like a tide. He felt a burning desire to travel, such as he had not felt since his youth. He felt that life had grown too narrow.

He was a famous man. He had been elevated by degrees—first to minor fame, then at last to official esteem. The royal highness of Bavaria had elevated him to the nobility. His works were studied in the schools. He had won prizes. A whole generation had grown up with his name.
And yet here he stood at the portal of a church feeling the longing of a schoolboy for somewhere else—anywhere else—for the warm south and the ancient sea.
He went home, looked at maps, and within the week had booked passage for Venice.

title: Chapter III: The Boy on the Beach
paragraphs: The weather when he arrived was not good. Venice lay under cloud, the canals were grey, and a smell of decay rose from the dark water. But Aschenbach went to the Lido and took rooms at the Grand Hôtel des Bains, and after two days the weather improved, and he was glad he had come.
He saw the boy on the third day.

He was perhaps fourteen—fifteen at the most. He was with a family—three girls, obviously his sisters, a governess, and a handsome mother dressed with quiet elegance. Aschenbach saw them at dinner, at adjacent tables, and noticed the boy at once.

He was extraordinarily beautiful. His face recalled the noblest moment of Greek sculpture—that pale, reserved perfection combined with sweetness, the expression of pure and simple humanity. He was dressed in a white suit with a blue-and-white striped linen shirt, and he moved with an unconscious grace that Aschenbach watched with a kind of dumbfounded admiration.

He told himself that the boy was simply a beautiful creature, as beautiful as a statue, and that he was observing him the way an artist observes beauty—dispassionately, intellectually. He was a writer, after all. He noticed beautiful things. There was nothing wrong with noticing.
His name was Tadzio. Aschenbach heard the governess call him by name on the beach, and the name, too, seemed to him beautiful—Tadzio—something antique and resonant, like a name out of Theocritus.

He watched the boy on the beach. He watched him run into the sea and emerge dripping and laughing. He watched him play with his companions and quarrel with his sisters. He watched him eat and read and sleep in his beach chair in the afternoon heat.

He told himself: this is beauty. This is what Plato meant—the beautiful as a manifestation of the divine, perceived through the senses. He was engaged in a kind of aesthetic contemplation. It was pure and philosophically defensible.
He did not admit to himself what else it was.

He was fifty-three years old and he had spent his whole life in rigorous self-discipline. He had written books about dignity and restraint and the artist's duty to master his passions. He had lived accordingly.
He told himself: I will leave tomorrow. Venice does not agree with my health. I will go somewhere else—the Austrian Tyrol, perhaps.
He went to the station and collected his luggage. He sat in the train car. The train moved.
And then, on the platform, he felt a violent, inexplicable grief—a grief like a physical blow—at the thought of never seeing Tadzio again.
He collected his luggage and returned to the hotel.

title: Chapter IV: The Plague
paragraphs: Venice had a secret, and the secret was dark.

Aschenbach noticed small things at first. The smell of disinfectant in unexpected places. A vendor's stall cleared away suddenly. A notice posted and then taken down. A conversation cut short when he approached.
He asked a travel agency clerk, indirectly.
'There is nothing to worry about,' the man said smoothly. 'Some cases of stomach trouble—the usual warm-weather illness. The city has taken measures.'
But Aschenbach's German newspaper contained a small item: 'Rumors of cholera in the Adriatic ports. Authorities advise caution.'

He walked through the city's narrow streets in the evening heat and smelled the warm stench of the canals and thought about cholera. He thought about the families on the Lido, the crowds at the beach. He thought about Tadzio.
He should tell them. The boy's mother—he should find her and tell her. There is disease in the city. Take your children and go.

He almost did it. He approached her once, on the terrace. She was sitting with her embroidery, and her composure—her beauty, her elegance—stopped him. He stood nearby and waited for an opportunity, and the words rose in his throat, and he swallowed them.
If he told her, she would leave. She would pack their things and take her daughters and Tadzio and go north, to some clean Austrian resort. And he would never see the boy again.
He said nothing.

He had read Plato. He knew what Plato said about beauty and about the beautiful lover. That beauty is a bridge—not an end in itself, but a way to the absolute. He had believed this. He had built his whole artistic life on this belief.

But here on the Lido, with the warm stinking wind off the diseased canals, he understood something different. That beauty is not always a bridge. That sometimes it is a snare. That the Platonic ascent—from the beautiful to the good to the true—requires a will toward goodness that can be corrupted, if the beautiful thing itself becomes the destination.
He watched Tadzio walk along the beach and felt the wreck of everything he had believed about himself.
He grew ill. The cholera—whatever was in the city's air and water—entered his blood. He sat in his beach chair and watched Tadzio for what would be the last time.

The boy walked to the edge of the water and stood there, knee-deep, looking out to sea. He raised one arm, pointing, gesturing outward. And to Aschenbach, already feverish, already dying, the gesture seemed to summon him—outward, into the immeasurable, into the absolute.
He tried to rise from his chair.
They found him a few minutes later, sitting very still, his head fallen to one side. His face had the stillness of a face that is done with all expression.
He was taken inside. By evening he was gone.
The news spread quickly through the hotel: the famous writer, Gustav von Aschenbach, had died of cholera on the Lido.
By then, the family from Poland had already gone. They had packed their things and departed that morning. Venice had been declared a public health risk.
Tadzio was somewhere to the north, on some green Austrian hillside, alive and unhurt, and probably thinking of nothing in particular.
